1: כִּי-תֵשֵׁב לִלְחוֹם אֶת-מוֹשֵׁל (when you sit down to eat with a ruler) — מוֹשֵׁל (môšēl, ruler, one who exercises authority) is the powerful host; בִּין תָּבִין (you will certainly understand/consider carefully) — the infinitive absolute with the imperfect for emphasis: understanding here is a command, not a suggestion. אֵת אֲשֶׁר-לְפָנֶיךָ (what is before you) — pānîm (face, before) is what confronts you directly; the advice is total situational awareness, not mere table manners. This opens the Thirty Sayings collection's first instruction on court behavior.
2: וְשַׂמְתָּ שַׂכִּין בְּלֹעֶךָ (put a knife to your throat) — שַׂכִּין (śakkîn, knife) appears only here in the Hebrew Bible; the word may be a borrowing. The violence of the image is deliberately hyperbolic: self-control in the ruler's presence requires something approaching self-force. אִם-בַּעַל נֶפֶשׁ אָתָּה (if you are a person of appetite/nephesh) — bʿal nephesh (master/owner of appetite) is the person driven by desire; here nephesh = appetite, the inner craving that must be disciplined at a powerful man's table where gratitude and power politics complicate ordinary hunger.
3: אַל-תִּתְאָו לְמַטְעַמֹּתָיו (do not crave his delicacies) — תָּאָה (tāʾâ, to long for, desire) and מַטְעַמֹּת (maṭʿammôt, delicacies, choice foods, literally "tastings") recall Esau's fatal appetite (Genesis 25:29–34). וְהוּא לֶחֶם כְּזָבִים (for it is deceptive bread) — כְּזָבִים (kəzābîm, lies, falsehoods) applied to food; the ruler's hospitality may contain unspoken obligations. The bread is a lure, not a gift.
4: אַל-תִּיגַע לְהַעֲשִׁיר (do not toil/wear yourself out to become rich) — יָגַע (yāgaʿ, to grow weary, to labor to exhaustion) frames the pursuit of wealth as a form of self-destruction through overwork. מִבִּינָתְךָ חֲדָל (cease from your understanding/wisdom) — the imperative ḥădal (desist, cease) is the call to stop; the phrase is compact: use your understanding (bînâ) to know when to quit the wealth-race. Wisdom here is self-regulating: it knows its own limits.
5: הֲתָעִיף עֵינֶיךָ בּוֹ וְאֵינֶנּוּ (you make your eyes fly at it, and it is gone) — עוּף (ʿûp̄, to fly) is used of the eyes darting after wealth; the irony is that wealth is the one that flies. כִּי-עָשֹׂה יַעֲשֶׂה לּוֹ כְנָפַיִם (for riches make wings for themselves) — the infinitive absolute ʿāśôh yaʿăśeh intensifies: money emphatically makes its own wings. כְּנֶשֶׁר יָעוּף הַשָּׁמָיִם (like an eagle it flies toward the sky) — the eagle image captures both swiftness and altitude: wealth disappears beyond recovery.
6: אַל-תִּלְחַם אֶת-לֶחֶם רַע עָיִן (do not eat the bread of an evil-eyed person) — רַע עַיִן (evil eye) is the Hebrew idiom for miserliness or stinginess (opposite of the generous "good eye," Proverbs 22:9). Eating with a miser is a social trap; his hospitality is not generosity but performance. וְאַל-תִּתְאָו לְמַטְעַמֹּתָיו (do not desire his delicacies) — the repetition of maṭʿammôt (from Proverbs 23:3) ties the instruction together: both the powerful ruler and the stingy man offer food that is not what it appears.
7: כִּי כְמוֹ-שָׁעַר בְּנַפְשׁוֹ כֵּן-הוּא (for as he calculates in his nephesh/mind, so he is) — שָׁעַר (šāʿar, to calculate, to estimate the price) is a commercial verb; the miser is always running a ledger in his inner being. nephesh here = the controlling inner self, the mind that determines behavior. אֱכֹל וּשְׁתֵה יֹאמַר לָךְ (eat and drink, he says to you) — the quoted speech contrasts with what he is actually thinking; his hospitality is verbal while his heart (lēb) is absent: וְלִבּוֹ בַּל-עִמָּךְ (his heart is not with you).
8: פִּתְּךָ-אָכַלְתָּ תְּקִיאֶנָּה (the bit you ate, you will vomit it up) — the physical imagery is deliberately crude: the body expels what cannot be truly received, food eaten under false pretenses that cannot be digested. וְשִׁחַתָּ דְבָרֶיךָ הַנְּעִימִים (and your pleasant words will be wasted/destroyed) — שָׁחַת (šāḥat, to destroy, ruin, corrupt) applied to speech; the pleasant conversation invested in the miser's table yields nothing. Both the food and the social capital evaporate.
9: בְּאָזְנֵי כְסִיל אַל-תְּדַבֵּר (in the ears of a fool do not speak) — the fool (כְּסִיל, kəsîl) is the one constitutionally closed to correction; the image of ears emphasizes that the problem is not acoustics but reception. כִּי-יָבוּז לְשֵׂכֶל מִלֶּיךָ (for he will despise the wisdom of your words) — בּוּז (bûz, to despise, hold in contempt) is the response of the mocker-fool to wisdom's content; wisdom is wasted on the one who despises it by constitution. The proverb counsels strategic silence: wisdom conserves itself.
10: אַל-תַּסֵּג גְּבוּל עוֹלָם (do not move the ancient boundary) — the repetition of Proverbs 22:28's phrase in a new context is significant; here the orphan (יְתוֹמִים, yətômîm) is the victim. וּבִשְׂדֵי יְתוֹמִים אַל-תָּבֹא (and do not enter the fields of orphans) — בּוֹא (bôʾ, to enter, come into) here means illegal encroachment on property. The orphan, like the widow, is the paradigmatic vulnerable person in Israel's law (Exodus 22:22; Deuteronomy 24:17), lacking legal advocates — which makes the following verse's assurance all the more powerful.
11: כִּי-גֹאֲלָם חָזָק הוּא (for their Redeemer is strong) — גֹּאֵל (gōʾēl, redeemer, kinsman-redeemer) is the legal-familial institution of Israel: the nearest male relative who steps in to restore what a family member has lost (land, freedom, life; cf. Leviticus 25:25–55; Ruth 2:20). Applied to YHWH, the institution is elevated into divine advocacy: God himself is the orphan's kinsman-redeemer. חָזָק הוּא (he is strong) — against the exploiter who counts on the orphan's legal helplessness, YHWH's strength reverses the power calculus. יָרִיב אֶת-רִיבָם אִתָּךְ (he will plead their case against you) — the courtroom language (rîb) of Proverbs 22 recurs.
12: הָבִיאָה לַמּוּסָר לִבֶּךָ (bring your heart to instruction) — the imperative הָבִיאָה (hābîʾâ, bring!) calls for an act of will: the student must direct the lēb toward מוּסָר (mûsār, discipline, instruction). This is not passive absorption but active redirection of the inner life. וְאָזְנֶיךָ לְאִמְרֵי-דָעַת (and your ears to words of knowledge) — the parallel pairs heart and ear: wisdom's formation works through both the volitional center and the auditory gateway, requiring the whole person.
13: אַל-תִּמְנַע מִנַּעַר מוּסָר (do not withhold discipline from a child) — מָנַע (mānaʿ, to withhold, restrain) implies that withholding correction is an active choice, not mere neglect; the parent must decide to apply the rod. כִּי-תַכֶּנּוּ בַשֵּׁבֶט לֹא יָמוּת (if you strike him with the rod he will not die) — the hyperbole counters overprotective hesitation; the feared outcome (death) is precisely what the rod prevents, not causes. The proverb addresses parental anxiety about harm through correction.
14: אַתָּה בַּשֵּׁבֶט תַּכֶּנּוּ (you will strike him with the rod) — the repetition of rod-discipline from Proverbs 23:13 is emphatic; Proverbs 23:13–14 form a paired instruction reinforcing each other. וְנַפְשׁוֹ מִשְּׁאוֹל תַּצִּיל (and his nephesh/life you will rescue from Sheol) — שְׁאוֹל (šəʾôl, the realm of the dead) frames the consequence without correction as death itself. nephesh here = life in the mortal sense; discipline is life-saving, not life-threatening. The rod is presented as an instrument of rescue, not punishment for its own sake.
15: בְּנִי אִם-חָכַם לִבֶּךָ (my son, if your heart is wise) — the father-son address (בְּנִי, bənî, my son) recurs here after the impersonal second-person instructions of the court-wisdom section; the tone becomes warmly personal. יִשְׂמַח לִבִּי גַם-אָנִי (my heart will rejoice, even I) — the emphatic gam-ʾānî (even I myself) expresses parental delight; wisdom in the son produces joy in the father's inner life. The proverb creates a moral community of gladness.
16: וְתַעְלֹזְנָה כִלְיוֹתַי (yes, my kidneys/inmost being will rejoice) — כִּלְיָה (kilyâ, kidney) is used in Hebrew as the seat of the deepest emotions and moral sensitivity (cf. Psalms 7:10; Proverbs 16:7; Proverbs 26:2); parallel to lēb (heart) as the center of inner life. בְּדַבֵּר שְׂפָתֶיךָ מֵישָׁרִים (when your lips speak what is right/straight things) — מֵישָׁרִים (mêšārîm, uprightness, right things) — plural noun of quality; the father's inner joy is specifically triggered by the child's righteous speech, the outward expression of inward wisdom.
17: אַל-יְקַנֵּא לִבְּךָ בַּחַטָּאִים (do not let your heart envy sinners) — קָנָא (qānāʾ, to be jealous, to envy) in the Piel; the heart is warned against the covert attraction to the apparent success of the wicked (cf. Psalms 37:1; Proverbs 73:2–3). כִּי אִם-בְּיִרְאַת יהוה כָּל-הַיּוֹם (but live in the fear of the LORD all the day) — kî ʾim introduces the positive alternative; continuous fear-of-the-LORD (kol-hayyôm, all the day) is the counterweight to the momentary attraction of sinners' prosperity.
18: כִּי אִם-יֵשׁ אַחֲרִית (for surely there is a future/end) — אַחֲרִית (ʾaḥărît, latter end, outcome, future) is the wisdom term for the eschatological horizon that gives present choices their weight (cf. Proverbs 23:14; Proverbs 24:14, 20). The fear of YHWH has a future; envy of sinners has no future that matters. וְתִקְוָתְךָ לֹא תִכָּרֵת (and your hope will not be cut off) — תִּקְוָה (tiqwâ, hope, expectation, a cord — same root as Rahab's scarlet thread in Joshua 2:18) will not be כָּרַת (kārat, cut off, severed). The hope is structural, not merely emotional.
19: שְׁמַע-אַתָּה בְנִי וַחֲכָם (listen, you — my son — and be wise) — the direct pronoun אַתָּה (ʾattā) is emphatic: this instruction is personally directed, not general. The father calls the son to two acts simultaneously: שְׁמַע (hear, attend) and חֲכַם (be wise, become wise) — hearing is not enough without wisdom's transformation. וְאַשֵּׁר בַּדֶּרֶךְ לִבֶּךָ (and keep/direct your heart on the path) — אָשַׁר (ʾāšar, to go straight, to lead straight) in the Piel; the heart must be actively directed rather than allowed to drift.
20: אַל-תְּהִי בְסֹבְאֵי-יַיִן (do not be among those who drink wine to excess) — סָבָא (sābāʾ, to drink heavily, to be a winebibber) — the participial form marks a habitual lifestyle, not an occasional indulgence. בְּזֹלְלֵי בָשָׂר לָמוֹ (among those who gorge themselves on meat) — זָלַל (zālal, to be light, worthless, to squander, to be a glutton) combined with bāśār (flesh, meat) describes the excessive consumption associated with feasting. The warning pairs wine-excess with meat-excess as twin paths to self-destruction (cf. Deuteronomy 21:20 for the "stubborn and rebellious son" charge).
21: כִּי-סֹבֵא וְזֹלֵל יִוָּרֵשׁ (for the drunkard and the glutton will come to poverty) — יִוָּרֵשׁ (yiwwārēš, Niphal of yārash, to be dispossessed, brought to poverty) — the passive of dispossession; the glutton is robbed of his own substance by his appetites. וּקְרָעִים תַּלְבִּישׁ נוּמָה (and drowsiness will clothe them in rags) — קְרָעִים (qərāʿîm, torn garments, rags) as the clothing of poverty; נוּמָה (nûmâ, drowsiness, slumber) personified as the one who clothes the idle in poverty's uniform. Sleep and drink conspire against the person.
22: שְׁמַע לְאָבִיךָ זֶה יְלָדֶךָ (listen to your father, this one who gave you life) — יָלַד (yālad, to beget, give birth) emphasizes the father as life-giver; the relative clause zeh yəlādekā (this one who begot you) grounds the command in creaturely origin. וְאַל-תָּבוּז לְאִמֶּךָ כִּי זָקֵנָה (and do not despise your mother when she grows old) — בּוּז (bûz, to despise, treat with contempt) is what one might do to the no-longer-powerful elder; the proverb names this tendency and forbids it. Age does not diminish the honor due to parents.
23: אֱמֶת קְנֵה וְאַל-תִּמְכֹּר (buy truth and do not sell it) — קָנָה (qānâ, to buy, acquire) — the commercial metaphor treats truth as a precious commodity worth market-price acquisition. אֶמֶת (ʾĕmet) has its root in the stability of something reliable, trustworthy, and enduring — not merely factual accuracy but ontological solidity. חׇכְמָה מוּסָר וּבִינָה (wisdom, instruction/discipline, and understanding) — the three cognitive-formational gifts of wisdom literature are listed as what one buys along with truth; they are not separate items but facets of truth's purchase price and yield.
24: גּוֹל יָגִיל אֲבִי צַדִּיק (greatly rejoice will the father of a righteous one) — גּוּל (gûl, to rejoice, exult) with the infinitive absolute gôl for emphasis; the father's joy is emphatic and complete. וְיוֹלֵד חָכָם וְיִשְׂמַח-בוֹ (the one who begets a wise son will rejoice in him) — wisdom in a child produces joy; the parent-child bond is the social site of wisdom's transmission and confirmation. The verse is the positive counterpart to the shame that a foolish son brings (Proverbs 10:1; Proverbs 17:21).
25: יִשְׂמַח-אָבִיךָ וְאִמֶּךָ (let your father and mother be glad) — the imperative is addressed to the son: make them glad through your wisdom. וְתָגֵל יוֹלַדְתֶּךָ (let her who bore you rejoice) — יָלַד returns; yôladtekā (the one who bore you) is an intimate designation for the mother. The father's joy is in the son's righteous character (Proverbs 23:24); the mother's joy is expressed here through the birthing relationship — you came from her, return her investment with wisdom.
26: תְּנָה-לִי לִבְּךָ בְּנִי (give me your heart, my son) — נָתַן (nātan, to give) applied to the lēb (heart): the father asks for the son's volitional center, not mere compliance. This is the deepest request of wisdom instruction — not behavioral conformity but inner orientation toward the teacher's wisdom. וְעֵינֶיךָ דְּרָכַי תִּרְצֶינָה (and let your eyes keep watch over my ways) — רָצָה (rāṣâ, to be pleased with, delight in, approve) here means more than passive observation; the son is to find delight in the teacher's path, aligning vision with value.
27: כִּי-שׁוּחָה עֲמֻקָּה זוֹנָה (for a prostitute is a deep pit) — שׁוּחָה (šûḥâ, pit, deep trench) recalls Proverbs 22:14's identical image for the adulterous woman's mouth; here the whole person of the זוֹנָה (zônâ, prostitute) is the pit. וּבְאֵר צָרָה נָכְרִיָּה (and the foreign/strange woman is a narrow well) — nākriyyâ (foreign, strange woman) is the moral outsider; בְּאֵר צָרָה (a narrow/constricted well) suggests a place that traps those who descend. Both images are spatial — downward movement, constriction, death.
28: אַף-הִיא כְּחֶתֶף תֶּאֱרֹב (indeed she lies in wait like a hunter/robber) — חֶתֶף (ḥetep̄, one who snatches, a robber) suggests predatory ambush; אָרַב (ʾārab, to lie in wait, to ambush) is the vocabulary of military ambush applied to seduction. וּבוֹגְדִים בְּאָדָם תּוֹסִף (and she increases the faithless/treacherous among men) — בָּגַד (bāgad, to deal treacherously, to be faithless) is covenant-betrayal language; she multiplies those who betray their covenantal commitments, turning men into oath-breakers.
29: לְמִי אוֹי לְמִי אֲבוֹי (who has woe, who has sorrow) — the catalogue of lemi (who has) questions is a rhetorical device listing the symptoms of wine's destruction: אוֹי (woe), אֲבוֹי (alas, lamentation), מִדְיָנִים (strife/quarreling), שִׂיחַ (complaining, anguish), פְּצָעִים חִנָּם (wounds without cause), חַכְלִילוּת עֵינַיִם (bleary/redness of eyes). The list is accumulative and clinical — a medical-social inventory of alcohol's consequences before the answer is given in Proverbs 23:30.
30: לַמְאַחֲרִים עַל-הַיַּיִן (those who linger over wine) — אָחַר (ʾāḥar, to delay, tarry, be late) applied to sitting with wine: the damage is not in a single cup but in extended lingering. לַבָּאִים לַחְקֹר מִמְסָךְ (those who come to investigate/seek out mixed wine) — מִמְסָךְ (mimsāk, spiced/mixed wine) is wine blended with spices for stronger effect. The one who searches out the strongest mixture is the answer to Proverbs 23:29's catalogue: the wine-obsessed is the source of all those symptoms.
31: אַל-תֵּרֶא יַיִן כִּי יִתְאַדָּם (do not look at wine when it turns red) — אָדַם (ʾādam, to be red) — wine's redness is its visual seductiveness; the prohibition begins with the gaze. כִּי-יִתֵּן בַּכּוֹס עֵינוֹ (when it gives its eye in the cup) — the wine is personified: it sparkles (ʿênô, its eye) in the cup, looking back at the drinker. יִתְהַלֵּךְ בְּמֵישָׁרִים (it goes down smoothly) — mêšārîm (uprightness, straight things) used ironically: what goes down straight destroys the person who swallows it straight.
32: אַחֲרִיתוֹ כְּנָחָשׁ יִשָּׁךְ (in the end it bites like a serpent) — אַחֲרִית (ʾaḥărît, the latter end, the outcome) — the wisdom term for eschatological consequence; wine's end is serpent-like. וּכְצִפְעֹנִי יַפְרִשׁ (and like a viper it spreads/strikes) — צִפְעֹנִי (ṣipʿônî, viper, adder) is a particularly dangerous snake; פָּרַשׁ (pāraš, to spread, to sting) suggests the poison dispersing through the body. The wine that looked like pleasure delivers venom as its true nature.
33: עֵינֶיךָ יִרְאוּ זָרוֹת (your eyes will see strange/foreign things) — זָרוֹת (zārôt, strange, foreign things) — hallucinations; the same word as the "strange woman" of the sexual danger passages; wine produces the same distorted perception. וְלִבְּךָ יְדַבֵּר תַּהְפֻּכוֹת (and your heart will speak perverse things) — תַּהְפֻּכוֹת (tahpukôt, perverse, twisted things; from hāp̄ak, to overturn) are what the intoxicated heart produces: speech that inverts right and wrong, wisdom and folly.
34: וְהָיִיתָ כְּשֹׁכֵב בְּלֶב-יָם (you will be like one lying down in the heart of the sea) — לֵב-יָם (heart of the sea) — the most dangerous place, exposed to all swells, with no stable ground; the drunk person has lost all orientation. וּכְשֹׁכֵב בְּרֹאשׁ חִבֵּל (like one lying on the top of a mast) — חִבֵּל (ḥibbēl, mast, rope?) — lying atop the ship's highest point in a storm is suicidal carelessness. Both images convey the drunk person's total loss of self-preservation instinct.
35: הִכּוּנִי בַל-חָלִיתִי ("they beat me but I felt no pain") — the drunkard speaks in first person for the first time; the quoted speech is darkly comic. The perfect verbs describe past beating (הִכּוּנִי) and insensibility (חָלִיתִי from ḥālâ, to be sick, to feel pain). מָתַי אָקִיץ אֲבַקְשֶׁנּוּ עוֹד ("when will I wake up so I can seek another drink?") — the final word is the tragic punchline: the cycle of intoxication, damage, and renewed craving is complete. Wisdom's portrait of the drunkard ends with his own voice asking for more poison.
